front: IDS stands for?
back: Intrusion Detection System

front: IPS stands for?
back: Intrusion Prevention System

front: What does an IDS do?
back: Monitors network traffic and system activity for malicious behavior or policy violations

front: What does an IPS do that an IDS does not?
back: Takes proactive action (blocks/quarantines) to prevent threats, not just detect them

front: How does an IDS respond to threats?
back: Passively — alerts, logs, notifies

front: How does an IPS respond to threats (beyond what an IDS does)?
back: Actively drops/blocks the malicious packets

front: Two deployment types of IDS?
back: Host-based and network-based

front: What is a host-based IDS installed on?
back: Individual local devices, for per-device protection

front: What is a honeypot?
back: A decoy system that mimics a real one to lure and trap attackers in an isolated, monitored environment

front: What do researchers extract from a honeypot?
back: Anomaly signatures (attacker behavior patterns)

front: Where can a network-based IDS sit relative to a switch, and why?
back: Beside the switch — it works on replicated traffic since it only needs to observe

front: Where must a network-based IPS sit, and why?
back: In the root of the traffic flow — it must respond in real time, not on a copy

front: Why does IPS require stricter configuration than IDS?
back: It actively blocks traffic, so misconfiguration disrupts legitimate communication

front: Connection-vs-security priority: how does a firewall differ from a physical separation hub?
back: Firewall puts connection first, security best-effort. Physical separation hub puts security first, connection second

front: What is the default-deny principle in firewall configuration?
back: Only traffic matching an explicit allow rule passes; everything else is blocked

front: Three techniques used in firewalls?
back: Packet filtering, stateful inspection, proxy firewall

front: Firewall technique: blocks based on predefined rules like source/destination IP?
back: Packet filtering

front: Firewall technique: makes decisions based on the state/history of the traffic?
back: Stateful inspection

front: Firewall technique: acts as a single point of contact with the outside, hiding internal IPs?
back: Proxy firewall

front: How does a proxy firewall protect internal machines from being targeted?
back: It hides their IP addresses behind itself, so attackers cannot address them directly

front: What attack is blocked when a proxy firewall only forwards completed 3-way handshakes?
back: SYN flood / half-open-handshake attacks

front: Main performance trade-off of running a firewall?
back: Slows network throughput; some legitimate traffic may be blocked

front: Downside of firewall configuration in practice?
back: Complex — needs an experienced network administrator; misconfiguration causes false positives
